design_philosophy.core_loop: Scout patrol patterns and environmental layout → Plan infiltration route → Execute stealth approach while managing visibility/audibility → Complete objective → Extract without detection
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Visibility versus audibility management; balance between shadow/silence and objective completion; consequence of detection (guards alert, mission failure on hard difficulty)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Master thief operating as a consummate professional; methodical planner who outsmarts rather than overpowers; urban infiltrator navigating a living steampunk city
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately anti-combat design (player easily defeated in direct confrontation); stealth is not optional stealth-action but mandatory stealth-first philosophy; hard difficulty makes human killing fatal to mission success; emphasis on methodical planning over reflexive action

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Three difficulty levels available; highest difficulty enforces ghost-play (no killing humans permitted); certain missions add additional restraints (cannot knock out guards); player can opt into harder constraints

progression.upgrade_system: Gear acquisition through theft-based economy; stolen valuables purchased between missions for specialized tools (arrows, gear); equipment variety unlocks tactical approaches
progression.unlock_mechanism: Progression through 15 sequential missions; objectives completed unlock story advancement and new mission locations; mission failure allows retry
replayability_hooks: Three difficulty levels with escalating constraints
Large level design supports multiple infiltration routes and approaches
Ghost-play constraint (no killing) on hard difficulty creates entirely different gameplay
Environmental puzzle variations based on patrol patterns

world_building_techniques: Environmental storytelling through level design (City district aesthetics, faction architecture)
Dialogue from guards and NPCs revealing world state and faction tensions
Mission briefing framing establishing objectives within conspiracy narrative
Faction visual language: Hammerite technology vs. Pagan naturalism vs. Keeper mystery
Temporal narrative (one year after first game; Trickster's defeat consequences; Mechanist schism consequences)

faction_indicators: Hammerites: theocratic order, martial discipline, servant language patterns
Mechanists: technological zealotry, industrial aesthetics, religious fervor directed toward progress
Pagans: nature worship, guerrilla tactics, wilderness aesthetics, spiritual mysticism
Keepers: enigmatic observation, manipulation, hidden knowledge
danger_signals: Resurgent police force equipped with Mechanist technology
Sheriff Gorman Truart pursuing Garrett (law enforcement agency operationalization)
Mechanist military capability creating security apparatus
Pagan guerrilla warfare response indicating faction desperation
Schism in Hammerite religion suggesting institutional instability
environmental_storytelling: City architecture: medieval-Victorian hybrid reflecting multiple eras coexisting
Mechanist facilities: industrial, automated, camera surveillance infrastructure
Pagan territories: wilderness beyond City, nature-integrated aesthetics
Hammerite temples: religious architecture, order and discipline reflected in design
Guard patrol patterns and positioning revealing faction control of districts

sources_used: wikipedia: Thief II - The Metal Age (gameplay section, plot/setting section)
